T21.claim: A humpback whale had me inside its mouth and spit me back out.
T21.shortExplanation: Packard was engulfed in a feeding humpback whale's mouth while diving off Cape Cod and was released alive.
T21.fullTruth: A crewmate witnessed the whale surface and expel Packard. Medical staff treated him after the encounter; he escaped without the catastrophic injuries he initially feared.
T21.context: Humpbacks cannot swallow a human through their narrow throat, but their enormous mouths can accidentally engulf a diver during feeding.
T21.transcript: A humpback whale had me inside its mouth and spit me back out.
T21.editorialBoundary: The claim means Packard was inside the whale's mouth, not swallowed into its stomach.
T21.lesson: A sensational sentence may become accurate once a key word—mouth versus stomach—is defined precisely.

sources.title: Lobster diver survives humpback whale encounter

T23.claim: I was trapped inside a hippo's mouth and survived repeated attacks.
T23.shortExplanation: A hippopotamus seized Templer repeatedly during a canoe safari on the Zambezi River, leaving him with dozens of major wounds.
T23.fullTruth: Templer survived a punctured lung, crushed and dislocated limbs, and roughly 38 serious wounds. Surgeons amputated his badly damaged left arm.
T23.context: The phrase sounds like an exaggeration, but Templer described being engulfed headfirst and released more than once.
T23.transcript: I was trapped inside a hippo's mouth and survived repeated attacks.
T23.editorialBoundary: A hippo could not swallow his entire body; the verified account is that much of his body was held inside its jaws and mouth.
T23.lesson: When wording sounds impossible, identify the literal boundary of the claim before rejecting the whole account.
